[[Stormtrooper/Legends|Stormtroopers]] were the remnant's foot [[Soldier/Legends|soldiers]],<ref name="Invasion 13">[[Invasion: Revelations 2|''Invasion: Revelations'' 2]]</ref> some of whom served in the [[Imperial Elite Guard]].<ref name="Invincible" /> The Remnant's elite [[Imperial shock trooper/Legends|shock troopers]] were said to be as good in combat as the legendary [[501st Legion/Legends|501st Legion]] that was historically employed by the Imperial [[Dark Lord of the Sith/Legends|Dark Lord of the Sith]], [[Anakin Skywalker/Legends|Darth Vader]].<ref name="Revelation" /> [[Scout trooper/Legends|scout troopers]], [[Hazard stormtrooper|hazard troopers]], [[Phase Zero dark trooper]]s, [[Cold assault stormtrooper|snowtroopers]], [[Jumptrooper/Legends|rocket troopers]], [[Imperial commando/Legends|Imperial commandos]] were also employed,<ref name="Jedi Academy">''[[Star Wars: Jedi Knight: Jedi Academy]]''</ref> and [[Imperial Navy Trooper/Legends|Navy troopers]] were utilized by the Imperial Navy.<ref name="Mercy Kill" /> [[DXR-6 disruptor rifle]]s were used by the Remnant's soldiers, [[Imperial Saboteur]]s were known to use them. Imperial soldiers, used [[E-11 blaster rifle/Legends|E-11 blaster rifles]], [[E-15 blaster rifle]]s, [[E-18 blaster rifle]]s, [[E-19 blaster rifle]]s, [[4-X blaster rifle]]s, [[rail detonator]] guns, which fired [[rail charge]]s, [[Imperial heavy repeater|heavy repeaters]], [[FC-1 flechette launcher/Legends|FC-1 flechette launchers]], [[ARC caster]]s, [[PLX-2M portable missile launcher]]s, [[Destructive Electromagnetic Pulse 2 ion carbine]]s, and [[Stouker concussion rifle]]s. [[E-Web heavy repeating blaster/Legends|E-Web heavy repeating blasters]] were also used. [[Thermal detonator/Legends|Thermal detonators]], [[Concussion grenade/Legends|concussion grenades]], and [[Laser trip mine/Legends|laser trip mines]] were used as well. Various [[Bioweapon/Legends|Bioweapons]], such as the [[Death Seed]] plague or [[nanovirus]]es, were also used. Before the [[Pellaeon–Gavrisom Treaty]], [[Cloaking device/Legends|cloaking technology]] was still used by the Imperial Remnant, a notable example was the [[Computerized Combat Predictor]], a failed data analyzer. In [[27 ABY/Legends|27 ABY]], Imperial technicians developed the [[gravitic amplitude modulator]], which was capable of controlling [[Gravity/Legends|gravity]] in a area. [[Mark III Merr-Sonn Defender Ion Mine|Defender Ion Mines]], and planetary [[Turbolaser/Legends|turbolaser]] batteries, [[ion cannon]]s, and planetary shield generators were used as orbital defense weapons, notably during the [[Battle of Borosk (Yuuzhan Vong War)|Battle of Borosk]].

==Society and culture==
{{Quote|We call it the Remnant, but to these people it's still the Empire.''"<br />"''An Empire without an Emperor.|[[Leia Organa Solo/Legends|Leia Organa Solo]] to [[Han Solo/Legends|Han Solo]]|<ref name="The New Jedi Order: Destiny's Way">''[[The New Jedi Order: Destiny's Way]]''</ref>}}
In the Imperial Remnant early [[Standard year/Legends|years]], [[Admiral/Legends|Admiral]] [[Gilad Pellaeon/Legends|Gilad Pellaeon]], called it the "true Empire" to distinguish it from remaining Imperial warlord factions,<ref name="TEA" /> but it was also known as "Pellaeon's Empire."<ref name="NEC">''[[The New Essential Chronology]]''</ref> Imperial and New Republic individuals alike also used the term "remnants of the Empire,"<ref name="Specter" /> which had previously been applied to the Galactic Empire in the years after [[Galactic Emperor/Legends|Galactic Emperor]] [[Palpatine/Legends|Palpatine's]] [[Death/Legends|death]] at the [[Battle of Endor/Legends|Battle of Endor]].<ref name="HTTESB">''[[Heir to the Empire Sourcebook]]''</ref><ref name="DESB" />

The Imperial Remnant was also known as simply the "Empire,"<ref name="Specter" /> and by [[43 ABY]], Imperial individuals had revived the term "Galactic Empire" once more, although "Imperial Remnant" was still the most common nomenclature used by the rest of [[The galaxy/Legends|the galaxy]].<ref name="Outcast">''[[Fate of the Jedi: Outcast]]''</ref> The Remnant was symbolically represented by the traditional [[Imperial crest/Legends|Imperial crest]].<ref name="Crimson Empire III 5">[[Crimson Empire III: Empire Lost 5|''Crimson Empire III: Empire Lost'' 5]]</ref>

==Astrography==
{{Main|Imperial Space}}
{{Quote|Can we hold on to our territory, though? The New Republic has perpetuated the lie that we rule by terror and force. Won't they insist on our destruction, treaty or not?''"<br />"''I don't think so. I believe we can convince even the most rabid of them that the worlds currently under Imperial rule remain with us by their own choice.|Moff [[Edan]] and Supreme Commander Pellaeon discuss the idea of a treaty with the New Republic|<ref name="Specter" />}}
[[File:Remnantspace.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Worlds under its control remained with the Imperial Remnant by their own choice.]]
Each sector in Imperial Space was governed by a Moff, and the systems within remained under Imperial rule by their own choice, preferring the efficiencies of Impeiral policy.<ref name="Specter" /><ref name="Empire Databank">{{DB|organization|theempire|Empire, the}}</ref> An exception, however, was when some systems were ceded to the Remnant by the Galactic Alliance in 41 ABY in return for aid lent during the Second Galactic Civil War.<ref name="Revelation" /> Economic development in the Remnant proceeded slowly in the years after the Bastion Accords. Only a few pockets of Imperial Space had amenities available that were comparable to those of the New Republic, and citizens of some worlds lived in grinding poverty. Industries were crippled due to the availability of cheap imported goods from the New Republic, which led to unrest and rioting.<ref name="Ruin" />

The Remnant's territory originally consisted of space that had previously belonged to the [[Pentastar Alignment]] and the other Imperial Rim factions that joined with Pellaeon to restore the Old Empire in 12 ABY.<ref name="TEA" /> Imperial territory stretched from [[Wild Space/Legends|Wild Space]] to the Mid Rim,<ref name="EC" /> but after seven years of losing ground to the New Republic,<ref name="Warfare" /> it had been reduced to eight sectors in the Outer Rim, consisting of over a thousand habitable [[Star system/Legends|systems]]<ref name="Specter" /> that all fell within former Pentastar Alignment space. The Remnant's borders remained as such until at least 35 ABY, but by 41 ABY, their territory had increased in size considerably, stretching through the Mid Rim and the [[Expansion Region/Legends|Expansion Region]] into the [[Inner Rim/Legends|Inner Rim]].<ref name="TEA" /> Following the Second Galactic Civil War, the Remnant's influence grew, as people across the galaxy appreciated its stability following several tumultuous years of Galactic Alliance rule.<ref name="FOTJ Dramatis Personae State of the Galaxy">''[[Fate of the Jedi: Dramatis Personae]]'' {{C|"The State of the Galaxy"}}</ref>

During Jagged Fel's reign as Imperial head of state, the Remnant believed that "Victory Without War" was the key to its future. The guiding philosophy was peaceful yet expansionist, and the Imperial Mission worked across the galaxy to aid impoverished worlds while simultaneously pushing the Imperial credo. Over time, the Remnant absorbed much of the Galactic Alliance's former territory.<ref name="TEA" />

==Behind the scenes==
Although the [[1995]] novel ''[[Ambush at Corellia]]'', set in [[18 ABY/Legends|18 ABY]], features Mara Jade musing that the Empire is dead with no "Imperial remnant" still remaining,<ref name="Ambush at Corellia">''[[Ambush at Corellia]]''</ref> the seeds of the Imperial Remnant were sown later in 1995 when Admiral Daala unified thirteen Warlord holdings and passed their command on to Gilad Pellaeon in [[Kevin J. Anderson]]'s novel [[Darksaber (novel)|''Darksaber'']], set six years before ''Ambush''.<ref name="Darksaber" /> A post-''Darksaber'' Empire was later referenced throughout [[1996]] and [[1997]] in several novels,<ref name="Before the Storm chapter 1">''[[Before the Storm]]''</ref><ref name="Shield of Lies">''[[Shield of Lies]]''</ref><ref name="Tyrant's Test chapter 4">''[[Tyrant's Test]]''</ref><ref name="The New Rebellion chapter 15">''[[The New Rebellion]]''</ref> and [[Timothy Zahn]]'s 1997 novel ''[[Specter of the Past]]'', set one year after ''Ambush'', depicted the Empire with Pellaeon as its leader.<ref name="Specter" /> The term "Imperial Remnant" was first used in the [[Union 1|opening issue]] of [[Michael A. Stackpole]]'s [[1999]]–[[2000]] [[Star Wars comic books|comic series]] ''[[Star Wars: Union]]'',<ref name="Union 1" /> although the Galactic Empire in the years after the Battle of Endor had been referred to as the "remnants of the Empire" as early as [[1992]]'s ''[[Heir to the Empire Sourcebook]]''.<ref name="HTTESB" />

[[File:Stackpoleinthe90s.jpg|thumb|left|200px|Michael A. Stackpole coined the term "Imperial Remnant."]]
Later sources, such as [[2009]]'s ''[[The Essential Atlas]]'', established that the Imperial Remnant was only born after the events of ''Darksaber'', when Pellaeon took Daala's unified Imperial forces out of the Deep Core and united them with various surviving Imperial factions in the Outer and Mid Rims.<ref name="TEA" /> [[2012]]'s ''[[The Essential Guide to Warfare]]'' confirmed that Daala's forces in ''Darksaber'' were a unique Imperial faction known as the "United Warlord Fleets."<ref name="Warfare" /><ref name="Warfare endnotes 16">{{Tumblr|subdomain=jasonfry|url=post/27417920547/eg-to-warfare-endnotes-pt-16|name=Jason Fry's Dorkery|text=EG to Warfare: Endnotes Pt. 16}}</ref> The first appearance of the Imperial Remnant can thus be said to be [[Barbara Hambly]]'s 1997 novel ''[[Planet of Twilight]]''—published several months before ''Specter of the Past''—which features an Imperial fleet belonging to Moff Tol Getelles and which references Pellaeon as the leader of the Empire.<ref name="Planet of Twilight">''[[Planet of Twilight]]''</ref> However, a Force-based [[Fallanassi/Legends|Fallanassi]] [[Mind trick/Legends|illusion]] of several Imperial Intelligence agents had previously appeared in 1996's ''[[Before the Storm]]'', set four years after ''Darksaber''.<ref name="Before the Storm chapter 12">''[[Before the Storm]]''</ref> The Remnant has since gone on to play large roles in such novel series as [[Star Wars: The New Jedi Order|''The New Jedi Order'']],<ref name="Ruin" /><ref name="The New Jedi Order: Force Heretic I: Remnant" /><ref name="Force Heretic Reunion">''[[The New Jedi Order: Force Heretic III: Reunion]]''</ref> ''[[Star Wars: Legacy of the Force]]'',<ref name="Revelation" /><ref name="Invincible" /> and [[Star Wars: Fate of the Jedi|''Fate of the Jedi'']].<ref name="Apocalypse" /><ref name="Outcast" /><ref name="Ascension" /> [[Elaine Cunningham]]'s novel ''[[Blood Oath]]'' was set to feature a plotline involving Jagged Fel and the Moff Council during Fel's early days as the Remnant's Head of State,<ref name="Blood Oath first look">{{SWArchive|url=vault/books/news20090106.html|text=First Look: ''Blood Oath''}}</ref> but the book was canceled by [[Del Rey]] and never published.<ref name="axed">{{SWBoards|url=message.jspa?messageID=17703919#17703919|thread=Books, Comics, & Television VIPs|user=[[Sue Rostoni]]|date=2009-08-13|time=6:41 PM}}</ref>

The Imperial Remnant's entry in [[2008]]'s ''[[The Complete Star Wars Encyclopedia]]'' asserts that Pellaeon's Empire did not adopt the name "Imperial Remnant" until after the signing of the Bastion Accords in 19 ABY,<ref name="CSWE">{{CSWECite|II|95|Imperial Remnant}}</ref> as does Pellaeon's entry in [[2002]]'s ''[[The New Essential Guide to Characters]]''.<ref name="NEGTC" /> However, this has been [[Retroactive continuity|retconned]] by more recent sources such as ''The Essential Atlas'' and ''The Essential Guide to Warfare'', both of which state that Pellaeon's Empire was known as the Imperial Remnant at the time of its birth in 12 ABY.<ref name="TEA" /><ref name="Warfare" /> Additionally, the comic series [[Star Wars: Crimson Empire III: Empire Lost|''Star Wars: Crimson Empire III—Empire Lost'']], released throughout [[2011]] and 2012, features characters who call Pellaeon's Empire the "Imperial Remnant" in 13 ABY.<ref name="Crimson Empire III 2" /> Although the 2002 [[Star Wars video games|video game]] ''[[Star Wars: Jedi Knight II: Jedi Outcast]]'' refers to its Imperial antagonists as the "Imperial Remnant,"<ref name="Jedi Outcast" /> they were later retconned into in fact being representatives of the Empire Reborn,<ref name="The Dark Forces Saga Part 6" /> an Imperial splinter faction first introduced in the [[1994]] novel ''[[The Crystal Star]]''.<ref name="The Crystal Star">''[[The Crystal Star]]''</ref>

The Remnant's entry in ''The Complete Star Wars Encyclopedia'' presents another discrepancy by captioning a picture of the Moffs of the Fel Empire as "Imperial Remnant leaders"<ref name="CSWE" />—the comic series ''[[Star Wars: Legacy]]'' had earlier confirmed that the Fel Empire was a unique successor state of the Remnant, created at some point before 127 ABY.<ref name="Legacy 0.5 1" /> ''Legacy''{{'s}} assertion of the Fel Empire as a unique state is confirmed elsewhere in ''The Complete Star Wars Encyclopedia''.<ref name="CSWE Imperial Missionaries">{{CSWECite|II|92|Imperial Missionaries}}</ref> Another error regarding the Remnant occurs in ''Warfare'', where a lone sentence mistakenly asserts that the Imperial Remnant participated in the [[post–Zsinj campaigns]] of [[8 ABY/Legends|8 ABY]].<ref name="Warfare" />
